title.en: Reviewing AI Code Like a Senior Engineer

description.en: Every time you accept an AI suggestion, you are doing a code review — and most people rubber-stamp it. Learn the concrete checklist a senior engineer runs on AI output: edge cases, error handling, security, spec match, hidden behavior, needless complexity, and the one question that catches the most bugs — 'do I understand every line?'

story_variants.en:
## Accepting Is Reviewing

Here is a fact most vibe coders never say out loud: **every time you hit 'Accept' on an AI suggestion, you just performed a code review.** You were the reviewer. You approved the change. And if you're honest, you probably approved it the way an exhausted teammate approves a 400-line pull request at 6pm on a Friday — a quick scroll, a vibe check, a thumbs up.

That is rubber-stamping, and it's the most common failure mode in AI-assisted development. The code looks clean, the variable names are sensible, it *reads* like something that works — so you wave it through. A senior engineer doesn't do that. A senior engineer reviews AI output the same way they'd review a brilliant, fast, overconfident junior who has never been on call: with respect for the speed and zero trust in the result.

## Reading for Intent, Thinking Like an Attacker

The junior reviewer reads code asking *"does this look like working code?"* The senior reviewer reads asking two very different questions:

1. **Intent:** *What is this actually supposed to do — and does this code do exactly that, no more, no less?* You compare the code against the spec in your head, not against your sense of style.
2. **Adversarial:** *How could this be wrong?* You actively hunt for the input that breaks it. You assume there is a bug and try to find it, instead of assuming it's fine and hoping.

That second mindset is the whole game. Happy-path code is easy to write and easy to approve. The bugs live at the edges — and AI is *especially* drawn to the happy path, because the happy path is what most code on the internet demonstrates.

## The Senior Review Checklist

Run this on every non-trivial AI snippet. It takes a minute and saves a 3am incident.

| Check | The question you ask | What you're hunting |
|---|---|---|
| Edge cases | What happens on empty / zero / negative / huge / `None`? | Crashes and wrong answers at the boundaries |
| Error handling | What happens on bad input — does it fail loud or fail silent? | Swallowed errors, bare `except`, wrong defaults |
| Security | Any injection, `eval`, secrets, unvalidated input? | The silent holes from the security lesson |
| Spec match | Does it do *exactly* what I asked — no more, no less? | Helpful extras you never wanted; missing requirements |
| Hidden behavior | Did it quietly change something I didn't ask it to? | Mutated inputs, changed defaults, reordered logic |
| Complexity | Is there a simpler version? Did it add a dependency? | Needless abstraction, a new library for a one-liner |
| Readability | Are the names honest? Will I understand this in a month? | Clever code that hides intent |
| Comprehension | **Do I understand every single line?** | The line you can't explain is the line that bites you |

The last row is the master check. If there is one line you cannot explain — a flag you didn't know existed, a regex you can't read, an API call you can't vouch for — **you have not reviewed the code. You have admired it.** Senior engineers refuse to approve code they don't understand, no matter how confident the author. The author here is a model that is confident about everything, including its mistakes.

## A One-Minute Review in Action

You ask for a function that returns what percent `part` is of `whole`. The AI hands you:

```python
def percentage(part, whole):
    return round(part / whole * 100, 2)
```

Rubber-stamp reaction: *clean, readable, ship it.* Senior reaction: run the checklist. **Edge cases — what is `whole == 0`?** `percentage(5, 0)` throws `ZeroDivisionError`. The AI answered the question you asked ("compute a percentage") and ignored the one you needed ("compute a percentage *safely*"). One row of the checklist, two seconds, caught a production crash:

```python
def percentage(part, whole):
    if whole == 0:
        return 0.0
    return round(part / whole * 100, 2)
```

Notice what happened. You didn't out-read the model on style — its style was fine. You out-reviewed it on **intent and edges.** That is the senior move, and it's a checklist, not a talent.

## Make the Checklist a Reflex

You will not run a formal eight-row review on a one-line rename. You *will* run it on anything that touches input, money, the database, the shell, or a user. The skill is knowing the difference, and then actually doing the pass instead of vibing. Accepting AI code is a code review. Start acting like the reviewer.

> **Key idea:** Hitting 'Accept' is approving a pull request, and most people rubber-stamp it. Review AI output like a senior engineer: read for intent, think adversarially about how it could be wrong, and run a concrete checklist — edge cases, error handling, security, spec match, hidden behavior, complexity, readability, and above all, 'do I understand every line?' The line you can't explain is the bug you'll ship.

left.label: Junior: 'looks clean, names are fine, ship it' — rubber-stamp the suggestion

right.label: Senior: 'how could this be wrong?' — run the checklist, find whole==0

description.en: Both readers see the same clean code. The senior reads for intent and thinks adversarially, asking what breaks it — and catches the ZeroDivisionError the junior approves straight into production.

executionSteps.description: Happy path works — the junior reviewer approves here.
Edge-case row of the checklist: whole==0 crashes. The senior caught it.

output: Hitting 'Accept' is approving a pull request.
Don't rubber-stamp it. Read for intent. Ask: how could this be wrong?
The line you can't explain is the bug you'll ship.

instruction.en: An AI wrote this percentage() function. It looks clean and the happy path works — but run the senior checklist's edge-case row: what happens when whole == 0? It throws ZeroDivisionError. Be the reviewer: fix it so a zero whole returns 0.0 instead of crashing, without changing the correct happy-path behavior. With the given data, percentage(5, 0) must return 0.0. Expected output: 0.0

en: Checklist row 1 — edge cases: ask 'what happens on zero?'. percentage(5, 0) divides by zero and raises ZeroDivisionError.
Guard the boundary before you divide: if whole == 0, return 0.0.
Keep the happy path identical: when whole != 0, still return round(part / whole * 100, 2).
Return the float 0.0 (not the int 0 or a string) so the type matches the normal result.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert percentage(5, 0) == 0.0, "whole == 0 must return 0.0, not crash"
assert percentage(25, 50) == 50.0, "happy path must be unchanged"
assert percentage(1, 3) == 33.33, "rounding to 2 dp must still work"
assert percentage(0, 0) == 0.0, "zero over zero is the edge case too"

q.en: What is actually happening every time you hit 'Accept' on an AI code suggestion?

en: Nothing — the AI already verified it
You are performing a code review and approving the change, whether or not you really reviewed it
The code is automatically tested before it lands
You are only saving a draft

q.en: Which question best captures the senior, adversarial way of reading AI code?

en: Does this look like working code?
Are the variable names pretty?
How could this be wrong — what input breaks it?
Did the AI sound confident?

q.en: On the review checklist, why is 'Do I understand every single line?' the master check?

en: Because long code is always wrong
Because a line you can't explain is one you can't vouch for — you admired the code instead of reviewing it
Because comments are required by Python
Because the AI hides bugs only in short lines

q.en: The AI's percentage() worked on (25, 50) but crashed on (5, 0). What does the senior reviewer conclude?

en: AI code is always broken and unusable
The AI answered the question asked ('compute a percentage') but not the one needed ('compute it safely') — so check the edges
Division should never be used
Percentages can't be computed in Python
